title: Welsh bara brith
subtitle: The speckled bread soaked overnight in tea
excerpt: A Welsh fruit loaf made by soaking dried fruit in strong tea overnight, then stirring in self-raising flour, an egg, and marmalade. No creaming, no complicated shaping: the fruit does the work.
sourceNotes: Florence White, Good Things in England (1932), documents Welsh bara brith in the regional baking chapter; the overnight tea-soak method is described as characteristic of the Welsh form as opposed to the yeasted Northumbrian version. The name means 'speckled bread' in Welsh. Pre-1928 Welsh Women's Institute recipe cards and the collection at the National Museum of Wales describe the same method.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice and freeze. Eat defrosted at room temperature or lightly toasted.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps for a week wrapped at room temperature; improves over the first few days as the fruit and tea flavours settle.

content.text: Bara brith is a tea loaf in the simplest sense: strong black tea plumps the dried fruit overnight, and that tea-soaked fruit gives the baked loaf its colour, moisture, and flavour. There is no butter in the recipe; the marmalade and the tea-swollen fruit do the job of fat and liquid together. The result is dense but not heavy, and keeps exceptionally well.

Brew 300 ml of strong black tea and pour it, still hot, over the dried fruit in a large bowl. Stir in the sugar. Cover and leave overnight at room temperature. By morning, the fruit will have absorbed most of the liquid and swollen considerably.

Heat the oven to 160°C fan. Grease and line a 900 g / 2 lb loaf tin. Stir the marmalade and beaten egg into the soaked fruit, mixing well. Sift over the flour and mixed spice and fold in until no dry flour remains. The batter is thick. Spoon into the prepared tin and level the top. Bake for 75 to 80 minutes, until a skewer through the centre comes out clean. Cover loosely with foil after 40 minutes if the top is darkening too fast. Cool in the tin for 15 minutes, then transfer to a wire rack. Wait until completely cold before slicing; the loaf slices cleanly once the crumb is set.
